ingredients.name: Pork shoulder or loin, thinly sliced
Beef chuck or sirloin, thinly sliced
Frozen spinach or fresh baby spinach
Large daikon radish, cut into rounds
Chicken thigh fillets, cut into 3cm chunks
Lamb shoulder or leg, thinly sliced
Pork loin or shoulder, thinly sliced
Chicken thigh fillets, cut into chunks
Sushi rice or white rice (raw weight)
Large frozen prawns, peeled and deveined, tails on
Lamb loin chops or shoulder, diced
White fish fillets (snapper, bream or flathead)

recipes.title: Teriyaki Chicken
recipes.description: Classic Japanese convenience food — sticky, savoury-sweet sauce that freezes and reheats beautifully.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make teriyaki sauce
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, garlic and ginger in a small saucepan over medium heat. Mix cornstarch with 2 tbsp cold water and stir in. Simmer until thickened, about 3–4 minutes. Set aside.

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.content: Slice chicken into thin fillets. Heat sesame oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Cook chicken 4–5 minutes per side until cooked through.

steps.title: Glaze and slice
steps.content: Pour teriyaki sauce over the cooked chicken in the pan. Toss to coat and cook 1–2 more minutes until glossy. Rest 5 minutes, then slice.

steps.title: Steam the broccolini
steps.content: Bring a small pot of salted water to a boil. Add broccolini and steam for 3–4 minutes until bright green and just tender. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice and glazed chicken across containers. Spoon any remaining sauce over the top. Steam broccolini for 3–4 minutes until just tender and add a portion to each container. Cool completely, then seal. Refrigerate up to 4 days or freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Mirin and sake are available at most Asian grocery stores and are worth getting — they make the sauce taste genuinely Japanese. Garnish with sesame seeds and sliced spring onion if you have them. Steam the broccolini for 3–4 minutes and serve alongside — it pairs perfectly with the teriyaki glaze.

recipes.title: Japanese Chicken Curry
recipes.description: Japanese curry is one of Japan's most beloved everyday meals — mild, thick, and deeply comforting. The sauce freezes exceptionally well.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Caramelise the onions
steps.content: Heat oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add onions and cook slowly, stirring often, until deeply golden and soft. This step builds the base flavour — don't rush it.

steps.title: Add chicken and veg
steps.content: Add chicken to the pot and brown on the outside, about 3 minutes. Add carrots, potatoes, and pour in stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.content: Simmer uncovered until carrots and potato are just tender.

steps.title: Add curry blocks
steps.content: Turn off heat. Break curry blocks into the pot and stir until fully dissolved. Add soy sauce. Turn heat back to low and stir until sauce thickens, about 5 minutes.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Portion rice and curry across containers. Cool completely before sealing. Refrigerate up to 4 days or freeze up to 3 months. Reheat curry in a pot or microwave with a splash of water to loosen.

recipes.notes: S&B Golden Curry blocks are sold at most Asian grocery stores and some supermarkets — they are the authentic shortcut used in Japanese homes. Medium heat is a good starting point. Freeze the curry and rice in separate containers if possible.

genki: S&B Golden Curry blocks (if not at Woolworths)

recipes.title: Salmon Miso Bowl
recipes.description: Covers your biggest nutritional gap — omega-3s, vitamin D, and healthy fats. Miso adds gut-friendly probiotics and a deep savoury flavour.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make miso marinade
steps.content: Whisk together miso paste, mirin, soy sauce, sake, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger in a bowl until smooth. Coat salmon fillets and marinate for at least 20 minutes.

steps.title: Bake the salmon
steps.content: Preheat oven to 200°C. Place marinated salmon on a lined baking tray and bake until just cooked through and slightly caramelised on top.

steps.title: Cook the edamame
steps.content: Boil or microwave edamame according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Add a salmon fillet and a portion of edamame to each. Sprinkle with sesame seeds. Cool completely before sealing. Freeze up to 2 months. Reheat gently — microwave at medium power to avoid drying the salmon out.

recipes.notes: Cook and freeze same day. The edamame adds protein, fibre, and iron. Miso paste keeps in the fridge for months — you'll use it again in other recipes.

fishmonger: Salmon fillets — ask if previously frozen. If not, freeze raw or cook first.

recipes.title: Tonjiru
recipes.description: A hearty, freezer-friendly Japanese pork and vegetable miso soup loaded with fibre, B vitamins, zinc, and iron. Eaten across Japan as an everyday staple.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Brown the pork
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a large pot over medium-high heat. Add pork and ginger and cook until the pork is no longer pink, about 3–4 minutes.

steps.title: Add vegetables and simmer
steps.content: Add carrots, daikon, potato, and shiitake to the pot. Pour in dashi stock. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a steady simmer until all vegetables are tender.

steps.title: Add miso
steps.content: Reduce heat to low. Scoop out a ladleful of broth and dissolve miso into it, then stir back into the pot. Do not boil after adding miso. Taste and adjust.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Ladle soup into containers and top with spring onions. Portion rice separately. Cool completely before sealing. Freeze up to 3 months. Reheat soup in a saucepan on low — avoid boiling to preserve the miso.

recipes.notes: Dashi stock powder (instant dashi) is the authentic base and available at Asian grocers. If daikon is hard to find, turnip works fine. Freeze the soup and rice separately.

recipes.title: Gyudon
recipes.description: Japanese beef and egg rice bowl — one of Japan's most iconic fast meals, eaten at Yoshinoya restaurants across the country. Eggs add choline, vitamin A, and fat-soluble vitamins.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Simmer onions in broth
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and dashi stock in a wide pan over medium heat. Add onions and simmer until soft and translucent, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Cook the beef
steps.content: Add beef to the pan, spreading it out in a single layer. Cook until just done, about 3–4 minutes. Don't overcook — the beef should be tender.

steps.title: Add the egg
steps.content: Whisk eggs and drizzle over the beef mixture. Cover with a lid and cook on low until the egg is just set.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Stir baby spinach or mizuna into the beef and onion mixture off the heat — it wilts in about 30 seconds. Divide rice across containers. Spoon beef, onion, egg, and wilted greens over each. Drizzle sesame oil over the top. Cool completely before sealing. Freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Ask your butcher for shabu-shabu style sliced beef if available — it's the authentic cut used in gyudon. Otherwise any thinly sliced beef works. Wilt a handful of baby spinach or mizuna into the broth just before portioning — it wilts in about 30 seconds and adds iron and colour.

recipes.title: Simmered Tofu & Vegetables
recipes.description: Japanese-style simmered tofu and vegetables in a light soy broth. Fills the vitamin C, magnesium, calcium, and plant fibre gaps that meat-heavy meals leave behind.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Press and prep the tofu
steps.content: If time allows, press tofu for 15–20 minutes to remove excess moisture. Wrap in a clean towel and place something heavy on top. Cut into 2cm cubes.

steps.title: Stir-fry the vegetables
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a large pan over medium heat. Add garlic and ginger, cook 1 minute. Add carrots, capsicum, and mushrooms and stir-fry for 3–4 minutes until slightly softened.

steps.title: Simmer in broth
steps.content: Pour in dashi stock, soy sauce, and mirin. Bring to a gentle simmer. Carefully add tofu and simmer for 10 minutes, letting the tofu absorb the broth.

steps.title: Add the spinach
steps.content: Stir in spinach and cook until just wilted, about 2 minutes.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Spoon tofu and vegetables over each with a ladle of broth. Sprinkle with sesame seeds. Cool completely before sealing. Freeze up to 2 months.

recipes.notes: Pressing the tofu improves texture dramatically and helps it absorb the broth. This meal is intentionally lighter and plant-forward to balance the heavier protein meals in your rotation.

recipes.title: Oyakodon
recipes.description: A Japanese comfort classic — chicken and egg simmered together in sweet dashi broth over rice. One of the most eaten home meals in Japan.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Simmer onions in broth
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a wide pan over medium heat. Add onions and simmer until soft and translucent, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Cook the chicken
steps.content: Add chicken thigh to the pan in a single layer. Cook until just done, about 5 minutes. Don't overcook — thigh stays juicy but can toughen if pushed too far.

steps.title: Add the egg
steps.content: Lightly beat eggs and pour two thirds over the chicken. Cover and cook on low for 90 seconds until just setting. Pour remaining egg over the top, cover and turn off heat. Residual heat finishes the egg.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Spoon chicken and egg mixture over each. Top with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Chicken thigh is used instead of breast — the higher fat content keeps it moist after freezing. Freeze same day. Reheat with a splash of water.

recipes.title: Butadon
recipes.description: Hokkaido's most famous bowl — sweet caramelised pork belly over rice in a sticky soy-mirin glaze. Richer and more indulgent than teriyaki.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Sear the pork
steps.content: Heat oil in a heavy pan over high heat. Add pork belly and sear until golden and slightly crispy on the outside, about 4–5 minutes. Drain excess fat if needed.

steps.title: Add the sauce
steps.content: Reduce heat to medium. Add garlic and ginger and cook 1 minute. Pour in soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar. Stir to combine.

steps.title: Reduce to a glaze
steps.content: Simmer uncovered, turning pork occasionally, until sauce reduces to a thick sticky glaze and coats the pork.

steps.title: Prepare the cabbage
steps.content: Finely shred green cabbage and toss with a small splash of rice vinegar and a pinch of salt. Set aside — it softens slightly as it sits and makes a great fresh contrast to the rich pork.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Spoon glazed pork over each and drizzle remaining sauce on top. Add a portion of shredded cabbage alongside the pork in each container. Garnish with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Pork belly has a higher fat content which makes it ideal for endurance training fuel and freezes exceptionally well — the fat keeps it moist after reheating. Pair this with a leaner meal in your weekly rotation. Shredded cabbage is the classic Butadon accompaniment — serve raw or lightly pickled with a splash of rice vinegar alongside the bowl.

recipes.title: Nikujaga
recipes.description: Japan's ultimate home-cooked beef stew — potato, onion, and beef simmered in a sweet soy-dashi broth. Often called Japan's national comfort food.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Brown the beef
steps.content: Heat oil in a large pot over medium-high heat. Add beef and brown lightly, about 3 minutes. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook the vegetables
steps.content: In the same pot add onions and cook until softened, about 4 minutes. Add carrots and potatoes and stir to combine.

steps.title: Simmer in broth
steps.content: Return beef to the pot. Pour in dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Bring to a boil then reduce to a steady simmer until potatoes are tender and broth has reduced slightly.

steps.title: Add the peas
steps.content: Stir in frozen peas and cook 2 more minutes.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Portion rice and stew into separate containers. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months. Reheat stew in a saucepan with a splash of water or microwave covered.

recipes.notes: A Japanese staple so fundamental that it's taught in school home economics classes across Japan. Freeze the stew and rice separately — potato texture holds better this way.

recipes.title: Shake Shioyaki
recipes.description: The simplest Japanese cooking technique — salt grilled salmon served over rice with pickled ginger. A staple of Japanese home cooking and convenience stores alike.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Salt the salmon
steps.content: Pat salmon dry with paper towel. Sprinkle salt generously over both sides of each fillet and drizzle with sake. Place on a rack uncovered in the fridge for at least 30 minutes — this draws out moisture and concentrates the flavour.

steps.title: Grill the salmon
steps.content: Pat salmon dry again with paper towel. Heat a non-stick pan or grill over medium-high heat. Cook salmon skin side down for 4 minutes until skin is crispy, then flip and cook 3 more minutes until just cooked through.

steps.title: Cook the edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions — usually 3–5 minutes in boiling water. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Place one salmon fillet on each. Add a small mound of pickled ginger and a portion of tsukemono alongside. Add a serve of edamame to each container. Sprinkle sesame seeds over the rice. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 2 months. Pack nori strips separately to add after reheating.

recipes.notes: Cook and freeze same day. This is one of the most eaten breakfasts and lunches in Japan — mastering it means you'll feel right at home. Add nori strips after reheating, not before freezing. Tsukemono (Japanese pickles) are the traditional accompaniment to shioyaki — pick up an assorted pack from Genki Mart. Steam edamame and serve on the side.

fishmonger: Salmon fillets — ask if previously frozen. If not, cook same day.

recipes.title: Saba Miso
recipes.description: Mackerel simmered in a rich miso and ginger broth — one of Japan's most nutritious and economical meals. Higher in omega-3s than salmon.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Score the mackerel
steps.content: Score the skin of each mackerel fillet with 2–3 shallow cuts. This stops the fish curling and helps the sauce penetrate.

steps.title: Make the broth
steps.content: Combine mirin, sake, soy sauce, sugar, and water in a wide pan over medium heat. Add sliced ginger and bring to a gentle simmer.

steps.title: Simmer the mackerel
steps.content: Place mackerel fillets skin side up in the simmering broth. Cover with a drop lid (otoshibuta) or a sheet of foil pressed directly onto the fish. Simmer gently.

steps.title: Add the miso
steps.content: Remove a ladleful of broth and whisk miso into it until smooth. Pour back into the pan and stir gently. Add halved baby bok choy and simmer uncovered for 3–4 more minutes until sauce thickens slightly and bok choy is just tender.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Carefully place a mackerel fillet on each, add a portion of bok choy, and spoon sauce generously over the top. Garnish with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 2 months.

recipes.notes: Mackerel is far cheaper than salmon in Australia and even cheaper in Japan. The ginger is essential as it neutralises the stronger fish flavour. Freeze same day, reheat gently at medium microwave power. Add halved baby bok choy to the simmering broth in the last 3 minutes — it absorbs the miso sauce beautifully.

fishmonger: Mackerel fillets — available at Asian fish markets or good fishmongers.

recipes.title: Ebi Chili
recipes.description: Japanese-style chilli prawns in a tangy tomato and chilli sauce — a popular Chinese-influenced dish that became a Japanese staple. High protein, low fat.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Prep the prawns
steps.content: Thaw frozen prawns if needed. Pat completely dry with paper towel — this is important for getting a good sear rather than steaming the prawns.

steps.title: Sear the prawns
steps.content: Heat vegetable oil in a large pan over high heat. Add prawns in a single layer and sear for 1 minute per side until just pink. Remove and set aside — they will finish cooking in the sauce.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.content: Reduce heat to medium. In the same pan add garlic, ginger, and toban djan and stir fry for 1 minute until fragrant. Add crushed tomatoes, soy sauce, sake, sugar, and chicken stock. Stir and simmer for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Thicken and finish
steps.content: Stir in cornstarch slurry and cook until sauce thickens, about 2 minutes. Return prawns to the pan and toss to coat. Drizzle sesame oil over the top.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Spoon prawn and sauce mixture over each. Garnish with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months. Reheat covered in microwave with a splash of water.

recipes.notes: Buy frozen prawns in 1kg bags from Woolworths — much better value. Toban djan is the key ingredient that makes this distinctly Japanese-Chinese. Substituting with sriracha works in a pinch but the flavour differs.

recipes.title: Tori Soboro
recipes.description: Seasoned ground chicken and scrambled egg served side by side over rice — a classic Japanese bento staple. One of the easiest meals you'll ever make.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Cook the chicken mince
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a pan over medium heat. Add ground chicken and ginger and break up with a spatula. Cook until no longer pink, about 4 minutes.

steps.title: Season the chicken
steps.content: Add soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar to the chicken. Stir continuously over medium heat until liquid is absorbed and chicken is glossy and slightly sticky. Set aside.

steps.title: Make the scrambled egg
steps.content: Whisk eggs with sugar and a pinch of salt. In a clean non-stick pan over low heat, add the egg mixture and stir constantly with chopsticks or a fork until fine, fluffy curds form. Remove from heat while still slightly soft.

steps.title: Cook the edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Spoon chicken mince on one half and egg on the other half. Scatter a portion of edamame across each container. Garnish with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: A classic Japanese bento staple — you'll see the two-colour chicken and egg presentation constantly in Japan. Freezes exceptionally well as both components are dry enough not to go soggy. Scatter steamed edamame alongside the chicken and egg for colour, fibre, and extra protein.

recipes.title: Oden
recipes.description: Japan's most famous hot pot — fishcakes, daikon, boiled egg, tofu, and konnyaku simmered in a light dashi broth. A winter staple sold at every convenience store in Japan.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Prep the konnyaku
steps.content: Bring a small pot of water to boil. Blanch konnyaku for 2 minutes to remove the strong smell. Drain and set aside. Score the surface lightly with a knife so the broth penetrates.

steps.title: Make the dashi broth
steps.content: Pour dashi stock into a large pot over medium heat. Add soy sauce, mirin, sake, and salt. Bring to a gentle simmer.

steps.title: Simmer everything
steps.content: Add daikon to the broth first as it takes longest. Simmer for 15 minutes, then add fishcakes, tofu, konnyaku, and boiled eggs. Simmer everything together gently.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Ladle oden ingredients and broth over each — one egg, one piece of each ingredient, and a generous pour of broth per container. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 2 months. Serve with a small dab of karashi mustard on the side.

recipes.notes: Oden is the most ingredient-intensive recipe in the rotation and requires a Genki Mart trip for most items — plan this one on a week when you're already going. Konnyaku, mixed fishcakes, and karashi are all essential to authentic oden. If konnyaku is unavailable, extra firm tofu works as a substitute. If you can only find one type of fishcake, chikuwa is the most important. Karashi mustard can be substituted with a small amount of regular hot mustard in a pinch.

recipes.title: Hijiki Gohan
recipes.description: Seasoned rice cooked with hijiki seaweed, carrot, and chicken — a traditional Japanese mixed rice dish packed with iodine, calcium, and iron.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Rehydrate the hijiki
steps.content: Soak dried hijiki in cold water for 20 minutes until rehydrated. Drain and squeeze out excess water.

steps.title: Cook chicken and vegetables
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a pan over medium heat. Add chicken thigh and cook until just done, about 5 minutes. Add carrots and rehydrated hijiki and stir fry for 2 minutes.

steps.title: Season and simmer
steps.content: Add crumbled tofu, soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and dashi stock to the pan. Stir and simmer until most of the liquid is absorbed, about 5 minutes. Add edamame and cook 1 more minute.

steps.title: Combine and portion
steps.content: Fold the hijiki and chicken mixture through the cooked rice gently until evenly combined. Sprinkle sesame seeds over the top. Divide across containers. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Hijiki is eaten regularly in Japanese school lunches for its mineral content — it's one of the most nutrient-dense ingredients in the entire rotation. Soak dried hijiki for 20 minutes before cooking. Only available at Asian grocers.

recipes.title: Yakitori Donburi
recipes.description: Grilled chicken skewers glazed in a sticky tare sauce, served over rice — izakaya style at home. One of the most iconic Japanese foods.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make the tare sauce
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and honey in a small saucepan over medium heat. Simmer until reduced by one third and slightly syrupy, about 8 minutes. This is your tare sauce. Set aside.

steps.title: Prep the chicken
steps.content: Thread chicken thigh chunks onto skewers if using, or leave as chunks for pan cooking. Brush lightly with sesame oil.

steps.title: Grill the chicken
steps.content: Heat a grill pan or regular pan over high heat. Cook chicken in batches, turning every 2 minutes, until cooked through and charred at the edges, about 8–10 minutes total. In the last few minutes add asparagus or shishito peppers to the pan with a brush of sesame oil and a pinch of salt — they char quickly alongside the chicken.

steps.title: Glaze with tare
steps.content: Brush cooked chicken generously with tare sauce and return to heat for 1 minute per side until the glaze caramelises. Repeat brushing once more off heat.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Arrange glazed chicken and grilled asparagus or shishito peppers over each. Drizzle remaining tare sauce on top. Sprinkle with sesame seeds, spring onions, and a pinch of shichimi togarashi. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Shichimi togarashi is a Japanese 7-spice blend used as a table condiment across Japan. Skewers are optional for batch cooking — a pan works just as well. Chicken thigh stays juicy under the grill and freezes better than breast. Grill asparagus or shishito peppers alongside the chicken — brush with a little sesame oil and a pinch of salt. They char beautifully on the same grill pan.

recipes.title: Jingisukan
recipes.description: Hokkaido's famous grilled lamb and vegetable dish named after Genghis Khan. Rich in iron, zinc, and omega-3s — the best fat profile in the entire rotation for endurance training.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Marinate the lamb
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, garlic, ginger, and sesame oil in a bowl. Add sliced lamb and toss to coat. Marinate for at least 20 minutes.

steps.title: Cook the vegetables
steps.content: Heat a wok or pan over high heat. Stir-fry onion rings and capsicum for 3–4 minutes until slightly charred. Add bean sprouts and toss for 1 more minute. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Grill the lamb
steps.content: In the same pan over high heat, cook marinated lamb in batches — don't overcrowd. 2–3 minutes per batch, turning once. Lamb should be just cooked with a little char.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Top with lamb and vegetables. Sprinkle sesame seeds over each. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months. Reheat with a splash of water.

recipes.notes: Jingisukan is named after Genghis Khan and is the defining dish of Hokkaido. The dome-shaped Jingisukan pan is traditionally used but a wok or flat pan works perfectly. Lamb is the only protein in the rotation with a genuine omega-3 profile comparable to salmon.

recipes.title: Yakisoba
recipes.description: Japan's most popular street food — stir-fried noodles with pork and vegetables in a tangy Worcestershire-style sauce. The only noodle meal in the rotation, adding real variety.

steps.title: Prep noodles and sauce
steps.content: If using fresh noodles, loosen them by hand or briefly blanch in boiling water for 30 seconds. Drain. Combine yakisoba sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce in a small bowl.

steps.title: Cook the pork
steps.content: Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Add pork and stir-fry until just cooked through, about 3–4 minutes. Push to one side.

steps.title: Add vegetables
steps.content: Add carrots and cabbage to the wok and stir-fry for 2 minutes. Add bean sprouts and toss everything together for 1 more minute.

steps.title: Add noodles and sauce
steps.content: Add noodles to the wok and pour sauce over everything. Toss vigorously over high heat until noodles are evenly coated and slightly caramelised, about 2–3 minutes.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide across 7 containers. Top each with benishoga and a sprinkle of aonori. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months. Reheat covered with a splash of water — noodles reheat better in a pan than microwave if possible.

recipes.notes: Yakisoba sauce is the authentic choice — find it at Genki Mart or use Worcestershire sauce as a substitute. Aonori and benishoga are traditional toppings but entirely optional — the dish is complete without them. If you skip the Genki Mart trip, Worcestershire + oyster + soy sauce is a perfectly good approximation. Fresh noodles are far better than dried for this dish.

recipes.title: Chicken Karaage
recipes.description: Japan's ultimate fried chicken — marinated in soy, ginger, and garlic then double-fried for an incredibly crispy exterior. The most popular izakaya dish in Japan. Freezes and reheats remarkably well.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Marinate the chicken
steps.content: Cut chicken thigh into 4cm chunks. Combine soy sauce, sake, ginger, garlic, and sesame oil. Add chicken, toss well and marinate for at least 30 minutes — longer is better.

steps.title: Cook edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Coat and first fry
steps.content: Remove chicken from marinade and coat thoroughly in potato starch. Heat oil to 170°C. Fry in batches for 3–4 minutes until pale golden. Remove and rest on a rack for 5 minutes.

steps.title: Second fry
steps.content: Increase oil to 190°C. Return chicken in batches for 1–2 minutes until deep golden and very crispy. The double fry is what makes karaage special — don't skip it.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Add karaage pieces and edamame to each. Cool completely before sealing — do not cover while hot or the crust will steam and go soggy. Freeze up to 3 months. Reheat in oven at 200°C for 12–15 minutes to restore crispiness.

recipes.notes: Potato starch gives a crispier result than cornstarch — worth getting from Genki Mart. Kewpie mayonnaise is the authentic Japanese dipping condiment and is a completely different product from Western mayo. The double fry technique is non-negotiable for proper karaage.

recipes.title: Buta Shogayaki
recipes.description: Ginger pork — one of Japan's most beloved everyday meals. Thin pork slices in a bold ginger and soy sauce glaze. Fast to cook, deeply satisfying, and a staple of Japanese home cooking and bento boxes.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make the ginger sauce
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and grated ginger in a bowl. Set aside.

steps.title: Prepare the cabbage
steps.content: Finely shred cabbage and toss with a pinch of salt. Set aside — it will soften slightly and serve as a fresh contrast to the rich pork.

steps.title: Cook the pork
steps.content: Heat oil in a pan over high heat. Cook pork slices in batches, 1–2 minutes per side until just cooked. Don't overcrowd — the pork should sear, not steam.

steps.title: Add the sauce
steps.content: Return all pork to the pan. Pour sauce over and toss over high heat for 1–2 minutes until sauce coats the pork and reduces to a sticky glaze.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Add glazed pork alongside a portion of shredded cabbage. Garnish with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Shogayaki is one of the most eaten lunches in Japan — you'll find it in every teishoku (set meal) restaurant. The cabbage is traditional and essential — its freshness cuts through the richness of the glaze. Ask your butcher for shabu-shabu style pork slices if available.

recipes.title: Sanma Shioyaki
recipes.description: Salt grilled Pacific saury — an autumn icon in Japan. Higher in omega-3s than salmon, incredibly cheap, and one of the most culturally significant fish in Japanese home cooking.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Salt the fish
steps.content: Pat sanma dry. Score the skin 2–3 times on each side. Salt generously on both sides and leave uncovered in the fridge for 30 minutes to draw out moisture.

steps.title: Cook edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Grill the sanma
steps.content: Pat fish dry again. Heat a grill pan or heavy pan over high heat. Grill skin side down for 4 minutes until skin is crispy and charred, then flip and grill 3 more minutes until cooked through.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Add a sanma fillet, a portion of edamame, and a mound of grated daikon to each. Add tsukemono alongside. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 2 months. Reheat gently at medium microwave power. Squeeze lemon and a dash of soy over after reheating.

recipes.notes: Sanma is available at Asian fish markets and good fishmongers in Australia, particularly in autumn. It is one of the most iconic seasonal foods in Japan — seeing sanma on the grill signals autumn has arrived. Grated daikon with soy sauce is the traditional condiment and cuts through the rich oily fish perfectly.

fishmonger: Pacific saury (sanma) — available at Asian fish markets. Mackerel is an acceptable substitute if unavailable.

recipes.title: Mapo Tofu
recipes.description: Japanese-style spicy tofu and pork mince in a deeply flavoured miso and chilli sauce. The Japanese version is milder and richer than the Sichuan original. High protein from both tofu and pork — the best plant-forward protein combination in the rotation.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Press the tofu
steps.content: Wrap tofu in a clean towel and press for 15 minutes to remove excess moisture. Cut into 2cm cubes.

steps.title: Cook the pork
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a wok over medium-high heat. Add garlic and ginger, stir-fry 30 seconds. Add pork mince and cook until no longer pink, breaking up with a spatula, about 4 minutes.

steps.title: Build the sauce
steps.content: Add toban djan and stir-fry 1 minute. Add soy sauce, sake, and chicken stock. Bring to a simmer. Dissolve miso in a ladleful of the broth and stir back in.

steps.title: Add tofu and thicken
steps.content: Gently add tofu cubes. Simmer 5 minutes. Stir in cornstarch slurry and cook until sauce thickens, about 2 minutes. Taste and adjust seasoning.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Ladle mapo tofu over each and garnish with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months. Reheat gently — avoid high microwave heat which can make the tofu rubbery.

recipes.notes: The Japanese version of mapo tofu uses miso for depth and is noticeably milder than the Sichuan original. Toban djan provides the heat — adjust to your spice preference. This dish is served at most Japanese Chinese restaurants and is a genuine home cooking staple.

recipes.title: Niku Udon
recipes.description: Thick udon noodles in a light dashi broth topped with sweetened beef — a Japanese comfort food staple. Adds a second noodle variety to the rotation with a completely different texture to yakisoba.

steps.title: Make the broth
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a large pot. Bring to a gentle simmer.

steps.title: Cook the beef topping
steps.content: In a separate small pan, combine 3 tbsp soy sauce, 2 tbsp mirin, 1 tbsp sugar. Add sliced beef and simmer over medium heat until beef is cooked and sauce is absorbed, about 5 minutes. Set aside.

steps.title: Cook the udon
steps.content: Cook frozen udon noodles in boiling water for 2–3 minutes until just tender. Drain and divide across 7 containers immediately — do not let them sit or they will clump.

steps.title: Assemble and finish
steps.content: Wilt baby spinach into the hot broth for 30 seconds. Ladle broth and spinach over each noodle container. Top with sweetened beef, narutomaki slices, and spring onions.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Sprinkle shichimi togarashi over each container. Cool completely with lids off — udon broth retains heat. Seal and freeze up to 3 months. Freeze noodles and broth together. Reheat in microwave covered with a splash of extra dashi or water.

recipes.notes: Narutomaki (the pink spiral fishcake) is the classic udon topping and available at Genki Mart, but it is purely a garnish — skip it entirely if unavailable and the dish is unchanged. Frozen udon noodles hold up well to freezing unlike most other noodles. Reheat covered with a splash of extra dashi or water.

recipes.title: Chicken Nanban
recipes.description: Miyazaki's famous fried chicken soaked in a sweet-sour nanban vinegar sauce and topped with tartar sauce. Named after the Southern Barbarians — Portuguese traders who brought frying to Japan in the 1600s. Rich, punchy, and completely unlike any other recipe in the rotation.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make nanban sauce
steps.content: Combine rice vinegar, soy sauce, and sugar in a small saucepan over low heat. Stir until sugar dissolves. Set aside in a wide shallow dish — the chicken will be soaked in this.

steps.title: Make tartar sauce
steps.content: Combine Kewpie mayo, finely chopped boiled eggs, and chopped pickles. Mix well. Refrigerate until needed.

steps.title: Coat and fry the chicken
steps.content: Season chicken thighs with salt. Dust in flour then dip in beaten egg. Heat oil to 170°C and fry in batches for 5–6 minutes until golden and cooked through.

steps.title: Soak in nanban sauce
steps.content: Immediately transfer hot fried chicken to the nanban sauce dish. Turn to coat and let soak for 2–3 minutes. The hot chicken absorbs the sauce as it sits.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Add baby spinach or cabbage to each. Place nanban-soaked chicken on top and spoon tartar sauce generously over. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months. Reheat in oven at 180°C for 15 minutes to restore texture.

recipes.notes: Chicken Nanban originates from Miyazaki Prefecture and is one of Japan's great regional dishes. The key is soaking the hot fried chicken straight into the nanban sauce — it creates a sticky, tangy glaze that permeates the coating. Kewpie mayo tartar is non-negotiable and completely different from Western tartar sauce.

recipes.title: Kani Tamago Don
recipes.description: Crab and egg rice bowl — essentially Oyakodon with kanikama instead of chicken. One of the fastest, cheapest, and most satisfying meals in the rotation. Kanikama is a staple of Japanese convenience store culture.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Cook edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Make the broth
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a wide pan over medium heat. Bring to a gentle simmer.

steps.title: Cook the kanikama
steps.content: Add shredded kanikama to the simmering broth. Cook for 2 minutes until heated through and the sauce has reduced slightly.

steps.title: Add the egg
steps.content: Lightly beat eggs and pour two thirds over the kanikama. Cover and cook on low for 90 seconds until just setting. Pour remaining egg over the top, cover and turn off heat. Residual heat finishes the egg — it should be just set, not rubbery. Drizzle sesame oil over.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Spoon kanikama and egg mixture over each. Add a portion of edamame alongside. Top with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months. Reheat with a splash of water.

recipes.notes: Kanikama is sold at Woolworths, Coles, and every Asian grocer. In Japan it costs almost nothing and is eaten straight from the pack as a snack. The technique here is identical to Oyakodon — once you've made both you'll have the egg-setting method locked in. Don't overcook the egg — the residual heat after turning off the stove is doing the work.

recipes.title: Buri Teriyaki
recipes.description: Yellowtail kingfish in a classic teriyaki glaze — the most eaten fish dish in Japanese home cooking. Richer and fattier than salmon, with a deeper flavour that takes the teriyaki glaze beautifully.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make teriyaki sauce
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a small saucepan over medium heat. Simmer until slightly thickened, about 3 minutes. Set aside.

steps.title: Steam the broccolini
steps.content: Steam broccolini for 3–4 minutes until bright green and just tender. Set aside.

steps.title: Cook the kingfish
steps.content: Pat fish dry. Heat oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Cook skin side down for 3–4 minutes until skin is golden and crispy. Flip and cook 2 more minutes until just cooked through.

steps.title: Glaze the fish
steps.content: Pour teriyaki sauce over fish in the pan. Spoon sauce over repeatedly for 1–2 minutes until fish is glazed and sauce is sticky.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Add glazed kingfish fillet and broccolini to each. Sprinkle sesame seeds and spring onions over. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 2 months. Reheat gently at medium microwave power.

recipes.notes: Yellowtail kingfish is sold as buri in Japanese supermarkets and is available at Australian fish markets and good fishmongers. It has more fat than salmon which makes it richer and more forgiving to cook — it's harder to overcook than leaner fish. Amberjack or hiramasa kingfish are interchangeable.

fishmonger: Yellowtail kingfish fillets — available at fish markets. Salmon is an acceptable substitute if unavailable.

recipes.title: Maguro Don
recipes.description: Sashimi-grade tuna over seasoned rice — one of the simplest Japanese meals you can make. Almost no cooking involved, just quality fish and a good sauce. A staple of Japanese fish market culture.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else. For a more authentic result, season the cooked rice with 2 tbsp rice vinegar, 1 tbsp sugar, and 1 tsp salt while still hot.

steps.title: Make the tuna marinade
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, and sesame oil in a bowl. Slice tuna into 1cm thick pieces. Toss gently to coat and marinate for 5 minutes — no longer or the fish will cure.

steps.title: Slice the cucumber
steps.content: Thinly slice cucumber and set aside. This adds freshness and crunch to each bowl.

steps.title: Portion and serve
steps.content: Divide rice across containers. Arrange tuna slices over rice. Add cucumber, pickled ginger, and nori strips. Sprinkle spring onions and sesame seeds over. Add a small dab of wasabi on the side. This dish is best eaten fresh — if meal prepping, keep tuna separate and add to rice just before eating. Do not freeze.

recipes.notes: Maguro Don is not freezer friendly — raw tuna should not be frozen and refrozen. This is your quick fresh meal for days when you want something fast and light. Sashimi-grade tuna is available at fish markets and good fishmongers — ask specifically for sashimi grade. Bluefin is best but yellowfin is excellent and more affordable.

fishmonger: Sashimi-grade tuna — must be sashimi grade, not regular fish counter tuna.

recipes.title: Whiting Fry
recipes.description: Crumbed and fried whiting fillets in the Japanese Aji Fry style — one of Japan's most popular teishoku dishes. Introduces the panko crumbing technique used across Japanese cooking. Whiting is the best Australian substitute for horse mackerel.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Prep the fish
steps.content: Pat fillets dry and season with salt. Set up a crumbing station — three shallow bowls with flour, beaten egg, and panko breadcrumbs.

steps.title: Crumb the fish
steps.content: Coat each fillet in flour, shake off excess. Dip in egg, then press firmly into panko on both sides. The panko crust should be even and thick.

steps.title: Fry the fish
steps.content: Heat oil to 170°C in a deep pan. Fry fillets in batches for 3–4 minutes until deep golden. Drain on a rack — not paper towel which makes the base soggy.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Add fried whiting and a portion of shredded cabbage to each. Add a small tub of tonkatsu sauce and Kewpie mayo on the side. Cool completely with lids off. Seal and freeze up to 3 months. Reheat in oven at 200°C for 12 minutes to restore crispiness.

recipes.notes: Panko breadcrumbs are the key difference between Japanese frying and Western — they are coarser, drier, and create a much lighter, crispier crust. Available at Woolworths in the Asian aisle. Tonkatsu sauce is the traditional accompaniment — find it at Genki Mart or use Worcestershire sauce. Draining on a rack rather than paper towel keeps the base crispy.

fishmonger: Whiting or flathead fillets — very affordable, widely available at fish markets.

recipes.title: Ebi Fry
recipes.description: Japanese crumbed prawns — a teishoku staple served alongside Aji Fry across Japan. Same panko technique as Whiting Fry but with prawns, which hold their shape differently and have a completely different texture.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Prep the prawns
steps.content: Thaw prawns completely and pat very dry. Make 3–4 shallow cuts along the belly of each prawn and press flat — this stops them curling during frying. Season lightly with salt.

steps.title: Crumb the prawns
steps.content: Set up crumbing station — flour, beaten egg, panko in three separate bowls. Coat each prawn in flour, dip in egg, then press firmly into panko. Hold by the tail while crumbing.

steps.title: Fry the prawns
steps.content: Heat oil to 170°C. Fry prawns in batches for 2–3 minutes until golden. They cook faster than fish — watch closely. Drain on a rack.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Add ebi fry and a portion of shredded cabbage. Add tonkatsu sauce and Kewpie mayo. Cool completely with lids off before sealing. Freeze up to 3 months. Reheat in oven at 200°C for 10 minutes.

recipes.notes: The belly-scoring technique is the key to straight ebi fry — Japanese restaurants score every single prawn. Prawns cook much faster than fish so keep the batches small and watch the colour. Ebi Fry and Whiting Fry are the two core fry dishes of Japanese teishoku and are often served together — once you have the panko technique down both are effortless.

recipes.title: Salmon Nanbanzuke
recipes.description: Fried salmon soaked in a sweet-sour nanban vinegar marinade with vegetables. The fish version of Chicken Nanban — lighter, more delicate, and a classic Japanese summer preservation dish. Develops in flavour over 24 hours.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make the nanban marinade
steps.content: Combine rice vinegar, soy sauce, sugar, and dashi stock in a saucepan over low heat. Stir until sugar dissolves. Pour into a wide dish. Add sliced onion, carrot, and chilli.

steps.title: Coat and fry the salmon
steps.content: Cut salmon into large chunks. Pat dry, season with salt, and dust in flour. Heat oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Fry salmon pieces for 2–3 minutes per side until golden and just cooked through.

steps.title: Marinate the salmon
steps.content: Transfer hot salmon immediately to the nanban marinade dish. Turn to coat and spoon marinade over. The hot fish absorbs the marinade as it cools. Rest for at least 15 minutes — overnight in the fridge is even better.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Add marinated salmon and a spoonful of the vegetable marinade over each. Garnish with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 2 months. The flavour intensifies after freezing and reheating.

recipes.notes: Nanbanzuke is a Japanese preservation technique — the vinegar marinade keeps the fish fresh and flavourful for longer than most fish preparations. It is traditionally served at room temperature or cold, making it one of the few fish dishes that actually works straight from the fridge without reheating. The longer it marinates the better it tastes.

fishmonger: Salmon fillets — fresh or previously frozen both work well.

recipes.title: Agedashi Tofu Don
recipes.description: Lightly fried tofu in a silky dashi broth served over rice. One of Japan's most elegant home dishes — crispy outside, silken inside, with a deeply savoury broth. A completely different tofu experience to simmered tofu.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Press and prep the tofu
steps.content: Press firm tofu for 20 minutes to remove excess moisture. Cut into large 4cm cubes. Dust thoroughly in potato starch on all sides.

steps.title: Cook edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Make the dashi broth
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a small saucepan. Bring to a gentle simmer and keep warm.

steps.title: Fry the tofu
steps.content: Heat oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Fry tofu cubes in batches for 2–3 minutes per side until golden and crispy. Drain on a rack.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Place fried tofu over rice and ladle warm dashi broth over each. Add edamame alongside. Top with spring onions, grated ginger, and sesame seeds. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 2 months. Reheat gently — the tofu softens in the broth after freezing which is still delicious.

recipes.notes: Potato starch gives a lighter, crispier coating than flour — essential for authentic agedashi tofu. The broth softens the crust after freezing but the flavour is unchanged. In Japan this is often served as a restaurant appetiser but makes a satisfying main over rice.

recipes.title: Kinoko Gohan
recipes.description: Japanese mushroom mixed rice — earthy, deeply savoury, and incredibly simple. Three varieties of mushroom cooked into seasoned rice with dashi. The mushroom version of Hijiki Gohan and one of Japan's great autumn comfort dishes.

steps.title: Prep the mushrooms
steps.content: Slice shiitake into strips. Trim enoki and shimeji into small clusters. Cube tofu. Cook edamame according to packet instructions and drain.

steps.title: Make the seasoning broth
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sake in a bowl. This will be used to cook the rice and mushrooms together.

steps.title: Cook mushrooms briefly
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Stir-fry all mushrooms and tofu for 3–4 minutes until mushrooms are wilted and slightly golden.

steps.title: Cook the rice with mushrooms
steps.content: Rinse rice and place in a pot. Add the seasoning broth plus enough water to total 900ml liquid. Add mushrooms and tofu on top — do not stir. Bring to boil, reduce to low, cover and cook until absorbed.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Fold edamame gently through the cooked mushroom rice. Divide across 7 containers. Top with spring onions and sesame seeds. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Enoki and shimeji mushrooms are available at Genki Mart and increasingly at Woolworths in the Asian section. If you can only find one variety, use shiitake — they carry the most flavour. Cooking the mushrooms directly in the rice infuses every grain with the earthy flavour.

recipes.title: Yaki Tofu Don
recipes.description: Grilled tofu in teriyaki glaze over rice — firm tofu charred on the outside, glazed with a sticky soy-mirin sauce. Completely different texture and flavour profile to both simmered and fried tofu.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Press and slice the tofu
steps.content: Press firm tofu for 20 minutes to remove excess moisture. Cut into thick 2cm slabs — not cubes. Pressing and slicing thick gives the best surface area for charring.

steps.title: Make teriyaki glaze
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a small pan over medium heat. Simmer until slightly thickened, about 3 minutes. Set aside.

steps.title: Steam the broccolini
steps.content: Steam broccolini for 3–4 minutes until bright green and just tender. Set aside.

steps.title: Grill the tofu
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a pan or grill pan over high heat. Add tofu slabs in a single layer. Cook without moving for 3–4 minutes until deeply golden and charred. Flip and repeat on the other side.

steps.title: Glaze and portion
steps.content: Pour teriyaki glaze over grilled tofu and toss to coat. Divide rice across 7 containers. Add glazed tofu slabs and broccolini to each. Top with spring onions and sesame seeds. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 2 months.

recipes.notes: The key to yaki tofu is patience — don't move the tofu while it grills or it will stick and break. A non-stick pan works but a cast iron or grill pan gives better char. The tofu needs to be very dry before hitting the pan — pressing for the full 20 minutes makes a significant difference.

recipes.title: Edamame and Egg Donburi
recipes.description: The fastest meal in the rotation — edamame and egg simmered in dashi broth over rice. High protein, minimal prep, and genuinely delicious. Inspired by the Japanese convenience store onsen tamago bowl.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Cook the edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions. If in pods, shell them after cooking. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Make the broth and add tofu
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a wide pan. Bring to a simmer. Add tofu cubes and edamame and cook for 2 minutes.

steps.title: Add the egg
steps.content: Beat eggs and pour two thirds over the edamame and tofu. Cover and cook on low for 90 seconds until just setting. Pour remaining egg over the top, cover and turn off heat. Rest 1 minute. Drizzle sesame oil over.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Spoon edamame, tofu, and egg mixture over each. Top with spring onions. Add nori strips to each container. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: This is your fastest cook — the edamame can be done the night before and refrigerated. Shelled edamame works much better here than pod edamame. The combination of edamame, tofu, and egg gives a surprisingly high protein count for a plant-forward meal.

recipes.title: Vegetable Tempura Don
recipes.description: Mixed vegetable tempura over rice in a sweet tsuyu sauce. Introduces the tempura batter technique — one of the most important cooking skills in Japanese cuisine. Lighter and crispier than Western frying.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Prep vegetables and tofu
steps.content: Slice sweet potato into 5mm rounds, zucchini into diagonal slices, cut tofu into thick rectangles. Pat everything very dry — moisture is the enemy of tempura batter.

steps.title: Cook edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Make tsuyu sauce
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a small saucepan. Bring to a simmer, stir until sugar dissolves. Keep warm.

steps.title: Make tempura batter
steps.content: Combine tempura flour and ice cold water in a bowl. Mix very briefly with chopsticks — lumps are fine and desirable. Do not overmix or the gluten will develop and the batter will be heavy. Use immediately.

steps.title: Fry the tempura
steps.content: Heat oil to 170°C. Dip vegetables and tofu in batter one piece at a time and fry in batches for 2–3 minutes until pale golden and crispy. Sweet potato takes slightly longer. Drain on a rack.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Add tempura pieces and edamame to each. Drizzle tsuyu sauce over the tempura. Top with spring onions. Cool completely with lids off before sealing. Freeze up to 2 months. Reheat in oven at 200°C for 12 minutes to restore crispiness.

recipes.notes: The two rules of tempura: ice cold water and do not overmix. Warm water activates gluten making the batter heavy and doughy. Lumps in the batter are correct. Tempura flour (available at Woolworths Asian aisle or Genki Mart) gives a lighter result than plain flour. This technique applies to all tempura — prawn, fish, and vegetable.

recipes.title: Tonkatsu Don
recipes.description: Crumbed pork cutlet and egg simmered in sweet dashi sauce over rice. One of Japan's most iconic dishes and the most eaten lunch in the country.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make the katsu sauce
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a wide pan over medium heat. Add thinly sliced onion and simmer until translucent, about 5 minutes. Keep warm.

steps.title: Crumb and fry the pork
steps.content: Pound pork loin to 1cm thickness. Season, coat in flour, dip in beaten egg, press into panko. Fry in oil at 170°C for 3–4 minutes per side until golden. Drain on a rack and slice into strips.

steps.title: Simmer katsu in sauce and add egg
steps.content: Place sliced tonkatsu into the simmering sauce. Beat remaining eggs and pour two thirds over the pork. Cover and cook on low 90 seconds. Pour remaining egg over, cover and turn off heat.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Spoon katsu, egg, and sauce over each. Garnish with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Katsudon is one of Japan's great comfort foods — eaten before exams for luck (katsu means to win). Pound the pork thin for even cooking. The egg and dashi sauce is what makes it special — the panko crust softens into the sauce beautifully.

recipes.title: Kakuni
recipes.description: Melt-in-your-mouth braised pork belly in a deeply rich soy and mirin sauce. Slow cooked but mostly passive. One of Japan's most celebrated pork dishes — the flavour is extraordinary.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Blanch the pork
steps.content: Cut pork belly into large 5cm blocks. Place in cold water, bring to a boil and cook 10 minutes. Drain and rinse — removes impurities and excess fat.

steps.title: Boil eggs
steps.content: Hard boil eggs for 8 minutes. Peel and set aside — they will be added to the braise to absorb the sauce.

steps.title: Braise the pork
steps.content: Return pork to the pot. Add soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, honey, ginger, and garlic. Add water to just cover. Bring to a boil, reduce to lowest heat, cover and braise for 90 minutes.

steps.title: Add eggs and reduce
steps.content: Add peeled boiled eggs. Braise another 30 minutes. Remove lid and simmer until sauce reduces to a sticky glaze, about 15 minutes.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Wilt spinach in remaining braising liquid. Divide rice across 7 containers. Add pork belly, a halved braised egg, and spinach to each. Spoon sauce over. Sprinkle sesame seeds. Freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Total cook time is 2.5 hours but active time is under 20 minutes — most is passive braising. The pork should collapse under light pressure when done. Cook this on a Saturday when you have time. The braised eggs absorb the soy-mirin sauce and are extraordinary.

recipes.title: Chashu Don
recipes.description: Ramen-style rolled pork belly over rice with a soft boiled marinated egg. The same chashu served on top of ramen across Japan, made into a donburi.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Roll and tie the pork
steps.content: Lay pork belly flat, roll tightly into a log and tie with kitchen twine at 3cm intervals. This gives the classic chashu slice shape.

steps.title: Make the braising liquid
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, honey, garlic, and ginger in a pot just large enough to hold the pork roll. Bring to a simmer.

steps.title: Braise the chashu
steps.content: Add pork roll to the braising liquid. Cover and braise on low heat, turning every 30 minutes to colour evenly, for 90 minutes total.

steps.title: Marinate the eggs
steps.content: Soft boil eggs for 6.5 minutes, peel and add to the braising liquid for the last 30 minutes. They turn golden and absorb the sauce.

steps.title: Slice and portion
steps.content: Remove twine and slice chashu into 1cm rounds. Reduce braising liquid over high heat until syrupy. Divide rice across 7 containers. Add chashu slices and halved egg. Drizzle reduced sauce over. Top with spring onions, sesame seeds, and nori. Freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: The soft boiled marinated eggs (ajitsuke tamago) are a revelation — jammy yolk soaked in soy-mirin. Once you master chashu you can top your own ramen. Kakuni and Chashu are both long braises — cooking both on the same day is efficient since they use the same technique.

recipes.title: Buta Kimchi
recipes.description: Pork and kimchi stir fry — one of the most popular home cooking dishes in Japan. Korean-influenced but completely adopted into Japanese food culture. Spicy, tangy, and takes 15 minutes.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Cook the pork
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a wok over high heat. Add pork slices in batches, 2 minutes per side. Push to one side.

steps.title: Add kimchi and sauce
steps.content: Add garlic and ginger, stir 30 seconds. Add kimchi and stir fry with pork for 2–3 minutes until kimchi caramelises slightly. Add soy sauce, sake, and sugar. Toss everything together.

steps.title: Add spinach
steps.content: Add spinach and toss for 30 seconds until just wilted.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Spoon buta kimchi over each. Top with spring onions and sesame seeds. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months. The kimchi flavour deepens after freezing.

recipes.notes: Aged kimchi (slightly sour) caramelises beautifully in the wok — better than fresh. Kimchi is available at Woolworths Asian aisle or Genki Mart. One of the first dishes many Japanese people learn to cook.

recipes.title: Buta Miso Itame
recipes.description: Pork and vegetable miso stir fry — one of the most frequently made home cooking dishes in Japan. Quick, uses pantry staples, deeply savoury. A true weeknight staple.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make the miso sauce
steps.content: Whisk together miso paste, mirin, sake, soy sauce, and sugar in a small bowl until smooth. Set aside.

steps.title: Cook the pork
steps.content: Heat vegetable oil in a wok over high heat. Add pork slices in batches, 1–2 minutes per side. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Stir fry vegetables
steps.content: Add sesame oil, garlic, and ginger. Stir 30 seconds. Add carrot and capsicum, stir fry 2 minutes. Add cabbage, stir fry 1 more minute.

steps.title: Add pork and sauce
steps.content: Return pork to the wok. Pour miso sauce over and toss over high heat for 1–2 minutes until evenly coated.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Spoon buta miso itame over each. Top with spring onions and sesame seeds. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: The miso sauce can be made in bulk and refrigerated for weeks — once you have it ready you can stir fry anything with it. Any vegetables work here — bean sprouts, zucchini, mushrooms are all common. This sauce is one of the most versatile things to know in Japanese home cooking.

recipes.title: Tamago Don
recipes.description: Egg and onion rice bowl in sweet dashi broth — the purest Japanese donburi. Eaten daily across Japan. Two eggs per serving makes it more protein-dense than it looks. One of the cheapest, fastest meals you can make.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Cook edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions. Drain and shell if in pods.

steps.title: Simmer onion in broth
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar in a wide pan. Add sliced onion and simmer until translucent, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Add the egg
steps.content: Beat eggs and pour two thirds over the onion. Cover and cook on low 90 seconds until just setting. Pour remaining egg over, cover and turn off heat. Drizzle sesame oil over. Rest 1 minute.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers. Spoon egg and onion over each. Add edamame alongside. Top with spring onions and nori strips. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: In Japan tamago don costs about 300 yen to make. University students and office workers eat it constantly. Two eggs per serving brings the protein up significantly — the edamame adds further protein and fibre. Essentially Oyakodon without the chicken.

recipes.title: Karei Nitsuke
recipes.description: White fish simmered in soy and mirin broth — one of Japan's most traditional home cooking fish dishes. Completely different technique to grilling or frying. Snapper or bream as the Australian substitute for flounder.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Score and prep the fish
steps.content: Score the skin of each fillet with 2–3 shallow cuts. Prevents curling and lets the sauce penetrate.

steps.title: Cook edamame
steps.content: Cook frozen edamame according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Make the simmering broth
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and sliced ginger in a wide pan. Add 200ml water. Bring to a simmer.

steps.title: Simmer the fish
steps.content: Place fish skin side up in the simmering broth. Cover with foil pressed directly onto the fish. Simmer gently for 8–10 minutes until just cooked. Spoon broth over occasionally.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Wilt spinach in remaining broth. Divide rice across 7 containers. Add fish, spinach, and edamame. Spoon broth generously over. Top with spring onions. Cool completely, seal and freeze up to 2 months.

recipes.notes: Snapper or bream work best — ask your fishmonger for the freshest white fish available. The pressed foil (otoshibuta) technique keeps the fish submerged in broth for even cooking. This simmering technique applies to any firm white fish and is one of the most useful Japanese cooking skills to know.

fishmonger: Snapper or bream fillets — any fresh white fish. Ask for the freshest available.

recipes.title: Ochazuke
recipes.description: Green tea poured over rice with salmon and pickles. One of Japan's great comfort foods — eaten after a long day or as a light meal. Takes 10 minutes and is one of the most culturally significant dishes you can learn.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Grill the salmon
steps.content: Season salmon with salt. Grill or pan fry over high heat for 3–4 minutes per side until just cooked. Flake into large pieces.

steps.title: Make the dashi tea broth
steps.content: Steep genmaicha tea bags in 500ml hot water for 2 minutes. Combine with dashi stock, soy sauce, and sake. Keep warm.

steps.title: Portion and serve fresh
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 bowls. Top with flaked salmon, tsukemono, pickled ginger, nori strips, spring onions, and sesame seeds. Pour warm dashi-tea broth over just before eating. Do not freeze — keep salmon and broth separate until serving.

recipes.notes: Not freezer friendly — best eaten fresh like Maguro Don. Genmaicha (roasted brown rice green tea) is traditional and gives a nutty warmth. Plain green tea or dashi alone both work fine. In Japan ochazuke packets are at every convenience store. Leftover rice is perfect for this.

recipes.title: Miso Ramen
recipes.description: A rich, home-style miso ramen — ground pork, corn, butter and a savoury miso broth over springy noodles. A genuine everyday noodle staple, not restaurant tonkotsu.

steps.title: Cook the noodles
steps.content: Bring a large pot of water to the boil. Cook ramen noodles according to packet instructions. Drain, rinse briefly in cold water to stop cooking, and set aside.

steps.title: Brown the pork
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a large pot. Add garlic and ginger, cook until fragrant. Add ground pork and cook, breaking it up, until browned through.

steps.title: Build the broth
steps.content: Pour in chicken stock, then whisk in miso paste and soy sauce until dissolved. Bring to a gentle simmer for 10 minutes — do not boil hard or the miso turns bitter.

steps.title: Add the corn
steps.content: Stir corn kernels into the broth and warm through for 2 minutes.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide noodles across 7 containers, ladle broth and pork over each, top with a small handful of raw bean sprouts, a knob of butter, and spring onion. Cool completely before sealing. Freeze the broth and noodles separately if possible — top with fresh bean sprouts and butter when reheating. Freeze up to 2 months.

recipes.notes: Home miso ramen won't taste like a 12-hour tonkotsu broth, and that's fine — this is the version people actually cook on a Tuesday night. The butter-and-corn combination is a genuine Hokkaido-style touch, not a gimmick.

recipes.title: Curry Udon
recipes.description: Thick udon noodles in a silky curry-spiced dashi broth — a beloved everyday noodle dish, and a great way to use the same curry blocks as your regular curry rice.

steps.title: Cook the noodles
steps.content: Cook frozen udon noodles according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Cook pork and onion
steps.content: Cook sliced pork and onion in a large pot over medium-high heat until pork is no longer pink and onion has softened.

steps.title: Build the curry broth
steps.content: Add dashi stock and bring to a simmer. Break in curry blocks and stir until dissolved, then stir in soy sauce. Simmer 10 minutes.

steps.title: Thicken the broth
steps.content: Mix cornstarch with a splash of cold water and stir into the broth. Simmer until slightly thickened and glossy.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide noodles across 7 containers and ladle the curry broth with pork over the top. Top with spring onion. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 2 months.

recipes.notes: Uses the same curry blocks as your regular curry rice — worth batching both on the same week if you're buying a box specially.

recipes.title: Yaki Udon
recipes.description: Stir-fried udon with pork and vegetables in a savoury soy-based sauce — a quick, everyday noodle dish, and a good contrast to Niku Udon's brothy style.

steps.title: Cook the noodles
steps.content: Cook frozen udon noodles according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Stir-fry the pork
steps.content: Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Add pork and stir-fry until just cooked through. Push to one side.

steps.title: Add the vegetables
steps.content: Add cabbage and carrot and stir-fry for 2 minutes. Add bean sprouts and toss for 1 more minute.

steps.title: Toss with the noodles
steps.content: Add noodles, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. Toss vigorously over high heat until everything is evenly coated, about 2 minutes.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide across 7 containers. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 3 months. Reheat with a splash of water — noodles reheat better in a pan than microwave if possible.

recipes.notes: A drier, wok-fried cousin to Niku Udon — good for weeks when you want noodles but not another brothy dish.

recipes.title: Tsukimi Udon
recipes.description: "Moon-viewing" udon in a simple soy-dashi broth topped with a soft egg — one of the simplest, most comforting everyday noodle bowls in Japan.

steps.title: Cook the noodles
steps.content: Cook frozen udon noodles according to packet instructions. Drain and set aside.

steps.title: Make the broth
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, and mirin in a pot. Bring to a gentle simmer.

steps.title: Warm the toppings
steps.content: Add fish cake slices and spinach to the broth, simmer 2 minutes until spinach wilts.

steps.title: Poach the eggs (fresh, per serving)
steps.content: This step is done fresh at serving time, not batched: crack an egg gently into the simmering broth in each individual bowl and cook until the white sets but the yolk stays soft, about 2-3 minutes.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide noodles, fish cake, and spinach across 7 containers with broth. Do not add the egg until reheating — cool the base completely, seal, and freeze up to 2 months. Top with spring onion and a fresh poached egg when reheating.

recipes.notes: The name comes from the egg yolk resembling the moon against the broth. Freeze the broth and noodles; always poach the egg fresh when you reheat.

recipes.title: Zaru Soba
recipes.description: Chilled soba noodles served with a cold dipping sauce — a genuine hot-weather everyday meal, and the only dish in the rotation that's meant to be eaten cold straight from the fridge.

steps.title: Cook and chill the noodles
steps.content: Boil soba noodles according to packet instructions. Drain and rinse thoroughly under cold running water while rubbing gently to remove surface starch, until fully chilled.

steps.title: Boil the eggs
