steps.content: Soft-boil eggs for 7 minutes, then cool in ice water and peel.

steps.title: Make the mentsuyu dipping sauce
steps.content: Combine dashi stock, soy sauce, and mirin in a small pot. Bring to a simmer for 3 minutes, then cool completely and refrigerate.

steps.title: Portion and chill
steps.content: Divide chilled noodles across 7 containers, top with nori strips and a halved egg. Store the dipping sauce separately with spring onion and wasabi on the side. Keep refrigerated, eat within 3-4 days — freezing isn't necessary since this keeps well chilled and needs no reheating.

recipes.notes: This one's eaten cold straight from the fridge — no reheating, no rice cooker phase. A nice change of pace in summer, and the fastest thing in the whole rotation to actually eat once it's made.

recipes.title: Yakimeshi
recipes.description: Japanese-style wok-fried rice with pork, egg, and vegetables — a genuine everyday household staple, and a completely different cooking technique to every donburi in the rotation.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else. Cool the cooked rice completely before frying — day-old, cold rice fries best.

steps.title: Cook the pork
steps.content: Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Cook diced pork until browned through. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Cook the vegetables
steps.content: In the same wok, cook garlic and carrot for 2 minutes. Add peas and cook 1 more minute.

steps.title: Scramble the egg
steps.content: Push vegetables aside, pour in beaten egg, and scramble until just set.

steps.title: Fry the rice
steps.content: Add cold cooked rice, breaking up any clumps. Return pork to the wok. Add soy sauce and sesame oil, and stir-fry over high heat until everything is well combined and the rice is lightly crisped.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide across 7 containers, top with spring onion. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Use rice that's been refrigerated overnight if you have it — fresh hot rice goes gluggy in a wok. Otherwise, spread freshly cooked rice on a tray to cool and dry out for 20 minutes before frying.

recipes.title: Omurice
recipes.description: Ketchup-fried chicken rice wrapped in a soft omelette — a beloved yōshoku household dish. Best saved for a weekend when you can make the omelette fresh rather than batch-cooked.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Cook chicken and onion
steps.content: Heat oil in a large pan. Cook diced chicken and onion until chicken is browned and onion has softened.

steps.title: Make the ketchup rice
steps.content: Add cooked rice and peas to the pan. Stir in ketchup and fry until the rice is evenly coated and lightly caramelised at the edges.

steps.title: Batch-cool the rice
steps.content: Divide the ketchup rice across 7 containers. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 2 months — this part batches perfectly.

steps.title: Make a fresh omelette per serving (at serving time)
steps.content: This step isn't batched — do it fresh each time you eat a portion. Whisk 2 eggs with a splash of milk. Melt butter in a small pan over medium heat, pour in egg, and swirl to a thin, soft omelette. Slide it over a reheated portion of ketchup rice and fold or wrap to cover.

recipes.notes: The rice batches and freezes beautifully, but the omelette really does need to be made fresh, one portion at a time — that's a real technique to practice, not a quick weeknight step, which is why this one's kept out of the regular rotation. Great for a lazy weekend breakfast.

recipes.title: Chicken Katsu Curry
recipes.description: Crispy fried chicken cutlet over curry rice — the iconic katsu-curry combination, and a katsu preparation for chicken rather than the usual pork.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Simmer the curry vegetables
steps.content: Combine onion, carrot, potato, and chicken stock in a pot. Bring to a boil, then simmer until vegetables are tender, about 15 minutes.

steps.title: Add the curry roux
steps.content: Remove from heat, break in curry blocks, and stir until fully dissolved. Simmer gently a few more minutes until thickened.

steps.title: Crumb the chicken
steps.content: Season chicken thighs with salt. Set up a crumbing station — flour, beaten egg, panko in three bowls. Coat each piece in flour, dip in egg, then press into panko.

steps.title: Fry the katsu
steps.content: Heat oil to 170°C. Fry chicken in batches for 4-5 minutes per side until deep golden and cooked through. Drain on a rack, then slice.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers, ladle curry over, and top with sliced katsu. Cool completely with lids off before sealing. Freeze up to 3 months. Reheat the katsu in the oven at 200°C for 12 minutes to restore crispiness before adding to the reheated curry rice.

recipes.notes: Keep the fried katsu separate from the curry when reheating if you want to preserve the crunch — a quick oven blast beats microwaving it in the sauce.

recipes.title: Hambāgu
recipes.description: Japanese-style hamburger steak in a ketchup-Worcestershire sauce — genuinely one of the most common household dinners in Japan, not a burger at all.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Soften the onion
steps.content: Finely dice onion and cook in a little oil over medium heat until soft and translucent. Cool slightly.

steps.title: Mix and shape the patties
steps.content: Soak panko in milk for a few minutes. Combine with ground beef, cooked onion, egg, and salt. Mix until just combined (don't overwork). Shape into 7 oval patties, tossing each between your hands to remove air pockets.

steps.title: Pan-fry the patties
steps.content: Heat a little oil in a pan over medium heat. Cook patties 4-5 minutes per side until well browned and cooked through. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Make the sauce
steps.content: In the same pan, combine ketchup, Worcestershire-style sauce, and a splash of water. Simmer 2 minutes, then swirl in butter to glaze.

steps.title: Steam the broccolini
steps.content: Steam broccolini for 3-4 minutes until just tender.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice, a patty, sauce, and broccolini across 7 containers. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: The milk-soaked panko is what keeps the patty soft rather than dense — don't skip it, and don't overwork the mince when mixing.

recipes.title: Korokke
recipes.description: Crispy panko-crumbed potato and beef croquettes — a hugely common household dish, and one of the best foods there is for freezing ahead.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Boil and mash the potato
steps.content: Boil potato until fork-tender, about 15 minutes. Drain well and mash until smooth.

steps.title: Cook the beef filling
steps.content: Melt butter in a pan, cook diced onion until soft, then add ground beef and salt and cook until browned. Combine with the mashed potato and mix well.

steps.title: Chill the mixture
steps.content: Spread the potato-beef mixture on a tray and refrigerate at least 30 minutes until firm enough to shape — this step matters, don't skip it.

steps.title: Shape and crumb
steps.content: Shape the chilled mixture into 14 oval patties. Coat each in flour, dip in beaten egg, then press into panko, covering evenly.

steps.title: Fry the croquettes
steps.content: Heat oil to 170°C. Fry croquettes in batches for 3-4 minutes until deep golden — they're already cooked inside, you're just crisping the crumb. Drain on a rack.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers with 2 croquettes each, shredded cabbage, and a small tub of tonkatsu sauce. Cool completely with lids off before sealing. Freeze up to 3 months. Reheat in the oven at 200°C for 12-15 minutes to restore crispiness.

recipes.notes: The most labour-intensive recipe in the book — boiling, mashing, chilling, shaping, and double-breading all add up. Worth batching a double portion of just the filling while you're at it, since it freezes raw just as well before crumbing.

recipes.title: Buri Daikon
recipes.description: Yellowtail simmered slowly with daikon in a sweet soy broth — a classic nimono technique, and a different way to cook buri to the grilled teriyaki style.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Parboil the daikon
steps.content: Boil daikon rounds for 10 minutes until slightly softened. Drain.

steps.title: Blanch the fish
steps.content: Briefly blanch buri fillets in boiling water for 1 minute to remove strong odour, then rinse in cold water.

steps.title: Simmer everything together
steps.content: Combine dashi, soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar, and ginger in a wide pot. Add daikon and buri. Bring to a simmer, cover with a drop-lid (or foil), and simmer gently for 20 minutes until the daikon is tender and the flavour has penetrated.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice, buri, and daikon across 7 containers, spoon the reduced sauce over the top, and garnish with spring onion. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 2 months.

recipes.notes: Blanching the fish first (a step called 'shimofuri') removes the strong fishy edge and is the difference between a good buri daikon and a great one — don't skip it.

recipes.title: Chikuzenni
recipes.description: Chicken and root vegetables simmered together in a sweet soy broth — a veg-forward, deeply traditional home-style simmer from Fukuoka.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Blanch the snow peas
steps.content: Blanch snow peas in boiling water for 1 minute, then plunge into cold water. Set aside for garnish.

steps.title: Brown the chicken
steps.content: Heat sesame oil in a large pot. Cook diced chicken until lightly browned on all sides.

steps.title: Add the vegetables
steps.content: Add lotus root, burdock, carrot, konnyaku, and shiitake. Stir-fry for 2-3 minutes until everything is coated in oil.

steps.title: Simmer
steps.content: Add dashi stock, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar. Bring to a boil, then reduce to a simmer, cover with a drop-lid, and cook for 20 minutes until vegetables are tender and the liquid has reduced to a light glaze.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice and the chicken-vegetable simmer across 7 containers, topping each with a few blanched snow peas. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: A genuinely traditional home-style dish with a lot more root vegetable variety than most of the rotation — a good one for a lighter, veg-heavy week.

recipes.title: Sukiyaki
recipes.description: Thin-sliced beef, tofu, and vegetables simmered in a sweet soy broth, traditionally dipped in raw egg — an iconic hot pot best saved for when you have people over.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Make the warishita sauce
steps.content: Combine soy sauce, mirin, sake, and sugar in a small pot. Warm gently until sugar dissolves, then set aside.

steps.title: Prepare the vegetables
steps.content: Wash and cut napa cabbage, shiitake, enoki, konnyaku, and onion into hot-pot-ready pieces. Arrange on a platter for easy access at the table.

steps.title: Cook in batches at the table
steps.content: In a wide, shallow pot (traditionally over a tabletop burner), simmer a portion of beef, tofu, and vegetables in the warishita sauce, adding more sauce and water as needed. Cook in batches rather than all at once for the best texture.

steps.title: Serve with a raw egg dip (fresh, at the table)
steps.content: Traditionally each diner cracks a raw egg into their own small bowl, beats it lightly, and dips each hot piece of beef and vegetable into it before eating. Serve alongside rice. Best cooked and eaten fresh at the table rather than portioned into containers — not really a batch-and-freeze dish.

recipes.notes: This is a table-cooked, share-style hot pot rather than a batch-prep meal — great for having people over, but it doesn't divide into weekly containers the way the rest of the rotation does, which is why it's excluded from auto-pairing by default.

recipes.title: Chinjao Rosu
recipes.description: Julienned beef and capsicum stir-fried in a glossy oyster-soy sauce — a Japanese-Chinese household staple, quick enough for any weeknight.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Marinate the beef
steps.content: Toss beef strips with cornstarch and soy sauce. Set aside for 10 minutes to tenderise.

steps.title: Sear the beef
steps.content: Heat oil in a wok over high heat. Stir-fry beef in batches until just browned. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Stir-fry the vegetables
steps.content: In the same wok, stir-fry garlic and ginger for 30 seconds, then add capsicum and bamboo shoots. Stir-fry for 2-3 minutes until just tender-crisp.

steps.title: Combine and sauce
steps.content: Return beef to the wok. Add oyster sauce, sake, and sesame oil. Toss everything together over high heat until glossy and combined.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice and the beef stir-fry across 7 containers. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Cutting the beef and capsicum into matching thin strips (not chunks) is what makes this dish — it cooks faster and looks the part too.

recipes.title: Yasai Itame Don
recipes.description: A mixed vegetable and tofu stir-fry bowl in a savoury garlic-soy sauce — a genuinely vegetarian option for weeks where you want to balance out the heavier meat dishes.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Pan-fry the tofu
steps.content: Heat oil in a wok over medium-high heat. Fry tofu cubes until golden on a couple of sides. Remove and set aside.

steps.title: Stir-fry the vegetables
steps.content: In the same wok, stir-fry garlic and ginger briefly, then add cabbage, carrot, capsicum, and mushrooms. Stir-fry for 3-4 minutes until just tender.

steps.title: Combine and sauce
steps.content: Return tofu to the wok, add bean sprouts, soy sauce, oyster sauce, and sesame oil. Toss over high heat until everything is well coated.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice and the vegetable-tofu stir-fry across 7 containers, sprinkle with sesame seeds. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Pan-frying the tofu first, rather than just tossing it in raw, is what keeps it from falling apart in the stir-fry.

recipes.title: Gyoza to Gohan
recipes.description: Pan-fried pork and cabbage dumplings, crisp on the bottom and steamed on top, served with rice — one of the most common weeknight dinners in Japan, and genuinely one of the best freezer foods there is.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Salt and drain the cabbage
steps.content: Finely chop cabbage, salt lightly, and let sit 10 minutes, then squeeze out as much liquid as possible — a watery filling makes for soggy gyoza.

steps.title: Mix the filling
steps.content: Combine ground pork, drained cabbage, garlic, ginger, spring onion, soy sauce, and sesame oil. Mix well until sticky and well combined.

steps.title: Wrap the gyoza
steps.content: Place a small spoonful of filling in the centre of each wrapper. Wet the edge, fold in half, and pleat to seal. This is the slow part — put on some music, it takes a while for a full batch.

steps.title: Pan-fry and steam
steps.content: Heat oil in a large flat-bottomed pan. Arrange gyoza flat-side down and fry until golden on the bottom, about 2 minutes. Add a splash of water, cover immediately, and steam for 4-5 minutes until the wrappers are translucent and cooked through. Uncover and let any remaining water evaporate to re-crisp the base.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice and gyoza across 7 containers with a small pot of soy sauce, rice vinegar, and chilli oil for dipping. For best results, freeze uncooked gyoza on a tray before fully assembling containers, then pan-fry straight from frozen when needed — cooked gyoza also freezes fine, just slightly softer on reheating. Freeze up to 2 months.

recipes.notes: Folding a full batch by hand does take a while — it's the slowest part of the whole rotation, not because it's difficult, just repetitive. Worth doubling the filling and freezing half uncooked while you're already set up.

recipes.title: Naporitan
recipes.description: Ketchup-fried spaghetti with sausage, onion, and capsicum — a beloved yōshoku pasta dish, and the first pasta in the rotation.

steps.title: Cook the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook spaghetti in salted boiling water until just past al dente (a little softer than usual is traditional here). Drain, tossing with a little oil to prevent sticking.

steps.title: Cook the vegetables and sausage
steps.content: Heat oil in a large pan. Cook sliced sausage, onion, capsicum, and mushroom until softened and lightly browned, about 6 minutes.

steps.title: Toss with the pasta
steps.content: Add cooked spaghetti and ketchup to the pan. Toss over medium-high heat until the pasta is evenly coated and the ketchup has caramelised slightly at the edges. Swirl in butter to finish.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide across 7 containers, top with a little grated parmesan. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 2 months. Reheat with a splash of water in a pan.

recipes.notes: The slightly overcooked, ketchup-caramelised spaghetti is the whole point — this isn't meant to taste like Italian pasta, it's its own household classic.

recipes.title: Ebi to Kinoko Wafu Pasta
recipes.title_jp: 海老ときのこ和風パスタ
recipes.description: Japanese-style pasta with prawns, mixed mushrooms, garlic, butter, and soy sauce — a genuine everyday weeknight staple in Japan, distinct from Italian pasta.

steps.title: Cook the spaghetti
steps.content: Cook spaghetti in salted boiling water until al dente. Reserve a cup of pasta water, then drain.

steps.title: Thaw and dry the prawns
steps.content: Thaw prawns completely and pat dry.

steps.title: Cook the garlic butter
steps.content: Melt butter in a large pan over medium heat. Add garlic and cook until fragrant, about 1 minute.

steps.title: Cook the mushrooms and prawns
steps.content: Add mushrooms and cook until softened, about 3 minutes. Add prawns and cook until just pink and curled, about 2-3 minutes.

steps.title: Toss with the pasta
steps.content: Add cooked spaghetti and soy sauce to the pan, along with a splash of the reserved pasta water. Toss until everything is evenly coated and glossy.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide across 7 containers, top with spring onion and nori strips. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 2 months. Reheat gently with a splash of water to loosen the sauce.

recipes.notes: Butter and soy sauce is a classic wafu combination — don't be tempted to add cream or cheese, that's a different dish entirely.

recipes.title: Hayashi Rice
recipes.description: Thin-sliced beef and onion in a rich tomato-demi sauce over rice — curry rice's yōshoku sibling, with a completely different sauce and flavour.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Brown the beef and onion
steps.content: Cook sliced beef and onion in a large pot over medium-high heat until beef is browned and onion softened, about 8 minutes.

steps.title: Make the roux
steps.content: Add mushrooms and cook 2 minutes. Push everything aside, melt butter in the cleared space, stir in flour, and cook 1-2 minutes to form a light roux, then stir through the rest of the pan.

steps.title: Simmer the sauce
steps.content: Add stock, crushed tomatoes, ketchup, and Worcestershire-style sauce. Bring to a boil, then simmer uncovered for 15-20 minutes until thickened to a stew-like consistency.

steps.title: Portion and freeze
steps.content: Divide rice across 7 containers and ladle the hayashi sauce over the top. Cool completely, seal, and freeze up to 3 months.

recipes.notes: Don't confuse this for curry — the tomato base gives it a completely different, slightly tangier flavour, closer to a light beef stew than a spiced curry.

recipes.title: Mizutaki
recipes.description: Chicken and vegetables simmered gently in a clear dashi broth, served with a ponzu dip — a classic household hot pot, best saved for when you have people over.

steps.title: Cook the rice
steps.content: Rinse the rice until the water runs clear, then cook it in a rice cooker (about 1:1.2 rice:water) — this takes ~50 minutes including resting, so start it before anything else.

steps.title: Blanch the chicken
steps.content: Briefly blanch chicken pieces in boiling water for 1-2 minutes to remove scum, then rinse.

steps.title: Start the broth
steps.content: Bring dashi stock to a simmer in a wide pot. Add the blanched chicken and simmer gently for 15 minutes until the chicken is cooked through and the broth is fragrant.

steps.title: Add the vegetables and tofu
steps.content: In batches, ideally at the table over a portable burner, add napa cabbage, tofu, shiitake, and carrot to the simmering broth, cooking each batch just until tender.

steps.title: Serve with ponzu (fresh, at the table)
steps.content: Serve each portion with ponzu sauce and spring onion on the side for dipping, alongside a bowl of rice. Best cooked and eaten fresh at the table rather than portioned into containers ahead of time — not really a batch-and-freeze dish.

recipes.notes: Like Sukiyaki, this is a share-style table hot pot rather than a batch-prep meal — great for guests, but it doesn't divide into weekly containers the way the rest of the rotation does, which is why it's excluded from auto-pairing by default.
